Despite differences in image orientations and timings, there is good qualitative correspondence between the 2 modalities (purple arrows). In both cases, it is clear the AVM is mainly supplied by the RPCA and branches of the RMCA (yellow arrows). Comparable visualization of venous drainage is also observed (orange arrows). Note that the color scheme used here differs from Figure 2 due to the different number of arterial components (see legend), and the sagittal x‐ray images have been rotated to try to match the VEPCASL image orientation more closely. All frames of the VEPCASL data can be viewed in Supporting Information Videos S2, S3, and S4
answers: A1: The image represents VEPCASL and X-ray DSA imaging modalities.

A2: The image depicts the area supplied by the right internal carotid artery (RICA) and one vertebral artery (VA), specifically regions supplied by the right middle cerebral artery (RMCA) and the posterior cerebral arteries (PCAs).
A3: Yes, the image identifies an Arteriovenous Malformation (AVM) that is mainly supplied by the right posterior cerebral artery (RPCA) and branches of the RMCA.
A4: The image shows irregularities as it identifies an arteriovenous malformation (AVM), which is an abnormal connection between arteries and veins, bypassing the capillary system.

A5: Yes, the image contains several significant labels or indices. Purple arrows correspond to the good qualitative correspondence between the two modalities, yellow arrows indicate the supply to the AVM, and orange arrows illustrate venous drainage. Differences in the color scheme used in the image and figure 2 are noted due to the different number of arterial components.
